sections.content: Nominal inflection is generally restricted to two categories—case and plurality—but kinship terms are uniquely inflected for inalienable possession (see appendix 2). Derivational affixes are also few in number.

sections.heading: Noun Inflection: Case and Plurality
sections.content:
Nouns in Skiri fall into three classes on the basis of their inflectional marking. There are two cases, instrumental ('with, using') and locative ('in, on, at; among'). Plural number is also marked for some nouns. Instrumental case is marked on all nouns—or at least on all those where it is semantically appropriate—by the suffix biriʔ. One class of nouns also uses biriʔ to mark locative case, as in iksiriʔ 'by hand; on the hand'. These nouns, which are mostly body part terms, mark plural number with the suffix -raar-, which occurs after the noun stem and before -biriʔ, as in ikstaaririʔ 'with the hands; on the hands'. Other classes of nouns have no special marking for plural, and mark locative case by either -ru or -kat (table 4).
In addition to its productive uses as instrumental and body-part locative, -biriʔ also occurs with a small set of noun stems, again primarily body part terms, to derive their independent forms; i.e., the locative form of these nouns is also the independent form, even though independent nouns are usually derived with the nominal absolutive suffix -uʔ (see below). Examples of locative forms serving also as absolutive forms are askatahciruʔ 'sole (of the foot)' and riitiriʔ 'small of the back'.
The suffix -ru 'among, in the country/territory of' forms the locative of tribal names and at least one state name. Examples are sahiiru 'in Cheyenne country' (< sahii 'Cheyenne'); pasaasiru 'among the Osages; in Osage territory' (< pasaasi 'Osage'); and tiksastu 'in Texas' (< English Texas). A variant of -ru is -wiru, which occurs with many names, usually ones ending in the vowel a, as uukaahpaawiru 'among the Quapaw' (< uukaahpaa 'Quapaw') and riihitawiru 'among the Ponca' (< riihita 'Ponca').
The locative suffix -kat generally translates as 'in, on' but with animate nouns it frequently translates as 'among'; e.g., akahkat 'on the dwelling' (< akar- + -kat); asaakat 'among the horses' (< asaa- 'horse' + -kat).

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: instrumental ('with, using'), locative ('in, on, at; among')
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Marks instrumental case on all nouns; marks locative case for one class of nouns (mostly body part terms); also occurs with a small set of noun stems (primarily body part terms) to derive independent forms.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs after the noun stem and before -biriʔ for body part terms.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Marks locative case for Tribal and Geographical Names.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Marks locative case for 'Other Nouns'; translates as 'in, on', but 'among' with animate nouns.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Usually used to derive independent nouns.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Variant of -ru, occurs with many names, usually those ending in the vowel 'a'.

tables.caption: Table 4. Skiri Case and Number Suffixes

sections.content:
The stem in the preceding form is a (ir-...) 'to come', and in it the preverb ir- (which has contracted with the aorist prefix uks-) has no independent meaning that relates to the activity of coming. Similarly, the root a has no independent meaning of 'come'.
In the following example the stem is the discontinuous form hiir (uur . . .) 'be good; be good-looking' and, as with a in the preceding example, the stem base hiir has no independent meaning.
tuuricaahiksii 'he is a good-looking person' < ti- IND + -Ø 3.A + uur- PREV + icaahiks- 'person' + hiir- 'be good; be good-looking' + -Ø PERF
wiitatuuhaaka 'we have done that' < wii- 'now' + ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + ut- PREV + raak- 1/2.P.A/P + aar 'do' + -Ø PERF
The preverb ut- is identical in form to the benefactive/dative ut-, and when a verb such as 'to do' has a benefactive patient, the benefactive is expressed by the sequence ir . . . ri . . . ut-, where the agentive possessive prefix ir- and the physical possessive prefix ri- combine with ut-, as in,
tatiriitaa 'I did it for him' < ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + ir- A.POSS + ri- PHY.POSS + ut- P.POSS + aar 'do' + -Ø

morphemes_mentioned.label: preverb
morphemes_mentioned.notes: contracted with aorist prefix uks-, no independent meaning related to 'coming'

morphemes_mentioned.label: agentive possessive prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: part of the ir . . . ri . . . ut- sequence for benefactive patient

morphemes_mentioned.label: physical possessive prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: part of the ir . . . ri . . . ut- sequence for benefactive patient

morphemes_mentioned.label: P.POSS
morphemes_mentioned.notes: part of the ir . . . ri . . . ut- sequence for benefactive patient

sections.heading: Verbs: Classes and Major Constituents
Inflectional Prefixes
sections.content:
Verbal prefixes fall into two groups: a large inner group whose members are more closely bound phonologically to one another and to the verb, and an outer group whose members are more loosely bound to the verb complex and can be classed more properly as proclitics, although most cannot occur independently.
9. What are here designated passive verbs are more generally termed stative verbs in other languages. Since Skiri does not have a prototypical passive voice and these verbs describe actions that befall subjects (who have no control over an event and are literally passive), and since there is another construction in Skiri that is more appropriately termed stative, the term passive seems most appropriate for this class of verbs.

sections.heading: GRAMMATICAL OVERVIEW
sections.content: The positions of the major inflectional constituents of the Skiri verb are portrayed in table 6.

tables.caption: Table 6. Major Positions in the Inflected Verb

sections.heading: Inner Prefixes
sections.content: The inner prefixes, like all verbal affixes, have a fixed position relative to one another. The inner prefixes include the following grammatical and semantic categories. (Numbers and categories in bold in the following table indicate the minimally required elements in a fully formed verb.)

tables.caption: Table 7. The Inner Prefix Template
rows: PATIENT PL, POSS PL, INDIR PAT PL

sections.heading: Mode.
sections.content: Every verb must be inflected for mode. There are nine non-subordinate (or independent) and four subordinate modes [table 7:10], one of which, the infinitive, is discontinuous [table 7:16]. Independent modes include the indicative, negative indicative, assertive, contingent, potential, absolutive, subjunctive, and infinitive,

sections.heading: Modal Prefixes
sections.content: while subordinate modes are the conditional, gerundial, subjunctive, and infinitive. The forms of the modal prefixes and their functions are given in Table 8.

morphemes_mentioned.label: Indicative modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 1st/2nd person, functions: statements, directive statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Indicative modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 3rd person, functions: statements, directive statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Negative Indicative modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 1st/2nd person, functions: negative statements, negative directive statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Negative Indicative modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 3rd person, functions: negative statements, negative directive statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Assertive modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, functions: emphatic statements, directive statements, demonstration/presentation statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Contingent modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 1st/2nd person, functions: future/resultative statements, sequential statements, mild imperative statements/requests

morphemes_mentioned.label: Contingent modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 3rd person, functions: future/resultative statements, sequential statements, mild imperative statements/requests

morphemes_mentioned.label: Potential modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 1st/3rd person, functions: future statements, statements of possibility/potentiality/probability

morphemes_mentioned.label: Potential modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 2nd person, functions: future statements, statements of possibility/potentiality/probability

morphemes_mentioned.label: Absolutive modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, functions: interrogative statements, statements in narration, demonstrative statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Subjunctive (Optative) modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 1st/2nd person, functions: statements of wish/desire

morphemes_mentioned.label: Subjunctive (Optative) modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate, 3rd person, functions: statements of wish/desire

morphemes_mentioned.label: Absolutive (Gerundial) modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Subordinate, functions: subordinate statements, gerundial forms

morphemes_mentioned.label: Subjunctive modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Subordinate, 1st/2nd person, functions: unrealized/hypothetical/contrary to fact statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Subjunctive modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Subordinate, 3rd person, functions: unrealized/hypothetical/contrary to fact statements

morphemes_mentioned.label: Infinitive modal prefix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Subordinate, functions: complement statements after verbs of wishing/thinking/feeling, subordinate statements, directive statements

tables.caption: Table 8. Modal Prefixes: Forms and Functions
rows: Unrealized, hypothetical, contrary to fact statements
Complement statements after verbs of wishing, thinking, feeling

sections.heading: GRAMMATICAL OVERVIEW
sections.content: them (individual animate objects)' < ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + ak- 3.AN.P + raar- 3.P.INDV.A + raspii 'look for' + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: Possession.
sections.content:
Both subject and object possession are marked by either single prefixes or combinations of prefixes. There is a distinction between regular and partitive possession, and between simple subject ownership and actual subject physical possession. Independent possessive constructions, which are gerundial forms of the verb 'to be one's' and serve as modifiers of nouns, are rarely used.

• ir . . . uk [ + ku-] 'agent possessor' (A.POSS) The construction that forms agent possession is ordinarily preceded by the proclitic ku- 'indefinite' [table 12:2] followed by the prefix ir- (first and second agent) or a- (third person agent) [table 7:13] and the verb stem uk 'be, exist' [table 7:26], as in the following example,

kutakawirahuʔ 'he owns that acre' < ku- INDF + ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + a- A.POSS + kawirahur 'be an acre' + uk 'be, exist' + -Ø PERF

• ir . . . ri . . . uur- 'physical agent possessor' (PHY.POSS) These three prefixes [table 7:13, 17, 18], when occurring with active verb stems designating positions and with certain locative and descriptive stems, translate as 'have, possess', as in,

taruuraariit 'he has it, possess it (something standing)' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + a- A.POSS + ri- PHY.POSS + uur- P.POSS + ariik 'be standing' + -Ø PERF

tatiriiriikstahka 'he keeps his arrows in it' < ta- IND.1/2A + t- 1.A + ir- A.POSS + ri- PHY.POSS + uur- P.POSS + riiks- 'arrow' + raar- PL + ka 'be in' + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: Patient Possession
sections.content:
uur- ‘patient possession’ (PHY.POSS). This morpheme occurs when someone other than the agent of the verb possesses a noun [table 7:18]. The pronominal patient occurring with the verb specifies the possessor. Illustrating its use is the following:
tatuuhkuutit aruusaʔ ‘I killed your horse’ < ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + a- 2.P + uur- PHY.POSS + kuutik ‘kill’ + -Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: patient possession
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs when someone other than the agent of the verb possesses a noun.

sections.heading: Benefaction
sections.content:
Benefaction. The morpheme ut- ‘benefactive; dative’ (BEN/DAT) marks a dative patient with non-discontinuous transitive verb stems [table 7:18], as in,
tatuutakusit ‘I picked it up for them’ < ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + ut- BEN + ak- PL.BEN + kusik ‘pick up’ + -Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: benefactive; dative
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Marks a dative patient with non-discontinuous transitive verb stems.

sections.heading: Evidentials
sections.content: Evidentials. Members of this category convey a speaker’s source of information or judgment of the validity of a statement, making explicit the kind of evidence supporting it [table 7:9, 14]. The five evidentials in Skiri are presented in table 11.

morphemes_mentioned.label: QUOT
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: Speaker is repeating what has been told

morphemes_mentioned.label: EV
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: Speaker has not witnessed an event, Speaker cannot see a designated object

morphemes_mentioned.label: EV
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: Speaker will not witness an event

morphemes_mentioned.label: EV
morphemes_mentioned.usage_contexts: Periodic past activity, usu. indef. time
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: Speaker describes an expected or normative activity

morphemes_mentioned.label: DUB.1
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: Speaker has not witnessed an event, Statement indicates element of doubt

morphemes_mentioned.label: DUB.2
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: Speaker has not witnessed an event, Statement indicates element of doubt

tables.caption: Table 11. Evidential Prefixes: Meanings and Distributions
rows: Speaker is repeating what has been told

Speaker has not
witnessed an event
Speaker cannot see a
designated object

Speaker will not
witness an event

Periodic past
activity, usu.
indef. time

Speaker describes an
expected or
normative activity

Speaker has not
witnessed an event

element of doubt

Speaker has not
witnessed an event
Statement indicates
element of doubt

sections.heading: Examples of verb forms with evidential prefixes
sections.content:
Examples of verb forms with evidential prefixes are the following:

witiʔitat ‘there was a village’ < wi- QUOT + ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + itak ‘be a village’ + -Ø PERF

kuubasuutiiʔit aruusaʔ ‘you must have seen the horse’ < kuur- DUB.1 + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + ut- PREV + iiʔirik ‘see’ + -Ø PERF

kusaahkikat ‘he might cry (at some future time when speaker will not see it)’ < kuus- POT.1/3.A + Ø- 3.A + i- SEQ + ar- EV + kikak ‘cry’ + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: Tense
sections.content:
As a grammatical category, tense is little developed in Skiri. Most verb forms are not marked for tense and translate temporally as either past or present or sometimes future. The potential mode and intentive aspect frequently translate into English as a future tense, but their meanings are wider than that. The one tense prefix is an aorist that is optionally used to indicate past time [table 7:20]. Its form is uks-, the same as the jussive prefix.

tatuksat ‘I went’ < ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + uks- AOR + at ‘go’ + -Ø PERF;
cp. taatat ‘I went’ < ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + at ‘go’ + -Ø PERF

tiibatuuksitkaspaawaa ‘I must have been eating in my sleep’ < tiir- INFR + ra- ABS + t- 1.A + uks- AOR + itkas ‘sleep’ + waawa- DIST + a ‘eat’ + -Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: AOR
morphemes_mentioned.notes: aorist prefix, same form as jussive prefix

sections.heading: Exhortation
sections.content:
Exhortation. There are many constructions that are used to form commands and requests of varying degrees of urgency.

*   The most common, and strongest, comprises the contingent mode and the jussive prefix uks- [7:20], which is identical in shape to the aorist prefix. It is a simple imperative that forms commands and is inflected for all personal agents. Examples are:
    sukskirikaʔaaʔ ‘wake up!’ < i- CONT.1/2.A + s- 2.A + uks- JUSS + kirikaʔaar ‘wake up’ + -Ø PERF
    ciksuhuurawaciiruutit ‘let’s go outside now!’ < i- CONT.1/2.A + acir- 1.DU.IN.A + uks- JUSS + uhuur- ‘finally’ + awacitik ‘go outside’ + ruu- DIST + -Ø PERF

*   Almost as common in usage as the preceding construction is the use of the contingent mode, often preceded by the hortatory (HORT) proclitic ki- [table 12:8]. It is a milder imperative that expresses exhortation, as in
    ruukiitaat ‘let me go there’ < ruu- ‘there’ + ki- HORT + i- CONT.1/2.A + t- 1.A + at ‘go’ + -Ø PERF
    kiʔiitiksbuubkaaʔ ‘let me dance now’ < ki- HORT + i- CONT.1/2.A + t- 1.A + ir- PREV + uks- JUSS + uhuur- ‘at last’ + kaaʔa ‘dance’ + -Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: JUSS
morphemes_mentioned.notes: jussive prefix, identical in shape to aorist prefix

sections.content:
Kiʔíisukskuutíirit cikstit. 'Take a good look at me!' < ki- HORT + i- CONT. 1/2.A + s- 2.A + uks- JUSS + ku- 1.P + ut- PREV + iirik 'see, look at' + -Ø PERF
Frequently the potential mode is used to admonish someone, as in
pahíituʔ kaasíkaa 'you must be quiet inside (there)' < pahiit- quiet; slow' + -uʔ NOM kaas- POT.2.A + s- 2.A + i- SEQ + kaa 'inside' + aah 'go around' + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: Directives or instructions
sections.content:
Directives or instructions are expressed by the indicative, negative indicative, and assertive modes with third person singular agent [table 7:10]. They are used, for example, when explaining to someone what one does in a certain situation or context or when telling someone how to make something.
kaakiiraakii 'don't be long' < kaaki- NEG.IND + Ø- 3.A + i- SEQ + raakiir 'be a long time' + -Ø PERF
kariiriiraakii 'don't be long!' < karíi- EMPH.NEG + rii- ASSR + Ø- 3.A + i- SEQ + raakiir 'be a long time' + -Ø PERF
Aaka, a raakutikuriiwictaawiiʔat. 'Oh, you should help me!' < . . . a 'and' + raa- 'just' + ku- INDF + ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV + ku- 1.P + ri- PHY.BEN + ut- PREV + i- SEQ + awaahc- 'at least' + raawiiʔat 'help' + -Ø PERF

The subordinate form of the infinitive mode with a third person agent is often used to implore, entreat, or admonish a person, as when urging someone to take a certain course of action or to refrain from certain behavior [table 7:10, 16]. Examples:
kararukuukikahu 'don't be drinking,' as when admonishing a person < kara- NEG + ra- INF.A + Ø- 3.A + ku- INF.B + i- SEQ + kiikaa 'drink' + -hus IMPF
Taku rakuukaksa rakuracakipíitit capáat. 'Someone (should) call out for the women to gather!' < ra- INF.A + Ø- 3.A + ku- INF.B + i- SEQ + kaksa 'call out' + -Ø SUB.4

sections.heading: Adverbials
sections.content:
Occurring after all prefixes except those for subject and object number and before the verb stem are two adverbial morphemes, uhuur- 'now, finally; a while' and awaahc- 'even, at least' [table 7:21]. An example is:
tuksuhuubwíitit 'let me sit down a while!' < i- CONT.1/2.A + t- 1.A + uks- JUSS + uhuur- 'a while' + wiítik 'sit down' + -Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: adverbial morpheme
morphemes_mentioned.slot: after all prefixes except subject/object number, before verb stem

morphemes_mentioned.label: adverbial morpheme
morphemes_mentioned.slot: after all prefixes except subject/object number, before verb stem

sections.heading: Outer Prefixes (Proclitics)
sections.content: The outer prefixes in Skiri are more properly designated as proclitics, since they are attached more loosely to the verb—that is, most do not generally undergo phonological processes that fuse them to one another or to the verb complex—but yet they are not independent morphemes.

tables.caption: Table 12. The Outer Prefix, or Proclitic, Template

sections.heading: Categories represented by proclitics
sections.content:
There are five categories represented by proclitics, as follows.

*   Reflexive and reciprocal (REFL). A single prefix witi- marks reflexive and reciprocal agents/patients [table 12:7], as in the following examples:
    *   wititaatacikstariku ‘I keep my thoughts to myself’ < witi- REFL + ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + acikstarikus ‘hold one’s thoughts (to oneself)’ + -Ø PERF
    *   siʔahawickaʔ siwitiirakuutiirit ‘they (DU) wanted to see each other’ < si- DU + ar- EV + ra- ABS + -Ø- 3.A + wicka ‘wish, want’ + -Ø PERF si- DU + witi- REFL + ra- INF.A + -Ø- 3.A + ku- INF.B + ut- PREV + iirik ‘see’ + -Ø PERF
*   Negatives. Although there is a negative indicative mode that parallels the indicative mode, other modes, including the indicative, require or optionally use one of several negative prefixes that precede them [table 7:8]. The negative prefixes, together with meaning and modal co-occurrence restrictions, are given in table 13.

tables.caption: Table 13. Negative Prefixes
rows: Indicative (+ wi- QUOT), Contingent, Absolutive, Conditional, Subjunctive, Infinitive

sections.heading: Interrogatives.
sections.content: There are six interrogative proclitics [table 12:8] that translate as, for example, ‘who’, ‘when’, ‘where’, ‘how many’, as well as form ‘yes-no’ questions. The two ‘yes-no’ interrogatives are more closely bound to the verb than are the other four forms. Those forms, together with modal co-occurrence restrictions, are presented in table 14.

sections.heading: Adverbials.
sections.content:
There are several adverbial proclitics that serve as introductory or transitional elements (translating as ‘then’, ‘and so’) and as temporal anchors (‘now’, ‘then’) [table 12:3, 5]. The most common are the following:
wii- ‘now; when’; also translates as English present perfective aspect, ‘have + -ed participle’
wiitahurabwiicaʔ ‘it is getting close now; it has come close now’ < wii- ‘now’ + ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV + huraah- ‘land’ + wiic- ‘moving close to a point’ + a ‘come’ + -Ø PERF
wiirakuhurabkiwatat ‘when the season changes’ < wii- ‘when’ + ra- INF.A + Ø- 3.A + ku- INF.B + huraar- ‘earth’ + kiwatak ‘change’ + -Ø SUB.4
kawiirasiiraʔ ‘have you come (i.e., arrived)?’ < ka- INTER + wii- ‘now’ + ra- ABS + s- 2.A + ir- PREV + a ‘come’ + -Ø PERF
ruu- ‘then; there’
ruuʔahiwakuʔ ‘then he said’ < ruu- ‘then’ + ar- EV + ri- CONT.3.A + Ø- 3.A + wakaʔu ‘say’ + -Ø PERF
wiiruutisakuriisat ‘then the sun set’ < wii- ‘now’ + ruu- ‘then’ + ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + sakur- ‘sun’ + iis- ‘out of sight’ + at ‘go’ + -Ø PERF
raa- ‘just’
raaruutikitaarit pah ‘the moon was just on top’ < raa- ‘just’ + ruu- ‘then’ + ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kitaarik ‘be standing on top’ + -Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: adverbial proclitic
morphemes_mentioned.notes: 'now; when', also translates as English present perfective aspect, 'have + -ed participle'

sections.heading: iriruu- 'thereupon'
sections.content:
iriruu- 'thereupon'
iriruuriíwuʔ 'thereupon they went' < iriruu- 'thereupon' + rii- ASSR + Ø- 3.A + wuu 'go PL.3.A' + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: Deictics
sections.content: Deictics. Finally there is a class of five deictics that indicate both temporal and spatial location [table 12:4]. They appear individually and in combinations. The most common ones are the following:

sections.heading: tii- 'this; here'
sections.content:
tii- 'this; here'
tiiʔahabaacca 'a log was lying here' < tii- 'here' + ar- EV + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + haak- 'wood' + sa 'be lying' + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: ii- 'that; there'
sections.content:
ii- 'that; there'
iiraaʔa 'when he came' < ii- 'then; when' + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV + a 'come' + -Ø SUB.4
iirahaacca 'that log (lying)' < ii- 'that' + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + haak- 'wood' + sa 'be lying' + -Ø SUB.4

sections.heading: iri- 'that (generalized)'
sections.content:
iri- 'that (generalized)' indicates that an object is located at an unspecified distance in space or time from the speaker, but is basically neutral to that distance.
iriwiitaasasaa 'you are named that' < iri- 'that' + wii- 'now' + ta- IND.- 1/2.A + s- 2.A + asaar 'be named' + Ø 'be'
iriwiituraahi 'that is good' < iri- 'that' + wii- 'now' + ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + uur- PREV + raahiir 'be good' + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: irii- 'what, where'
sections.content:
irii- 'what, where'
ituuhaarikaaʔ 'I'll tell you what to do' < i- CONT.1/2.A + t- 1.A + a- 2.P + ir- PREV + ut- PREV + raarikaaʔa 'to tell to' + -Ø PERF
iriiʔahaarasika icaas 'where he cooked Turtle' < irii- 'where' + ar- EV + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + arasik 'cook' + -a SUB.1
tiiʔiriirasku 'here where you are' < tii- 'here' + irii- 'where' + ra- ABS + s- 2.A + kus 'be sitting' + -Ø SUB.4

sections.heading: riku- 'the one that/who; what'
sections.content:
riku- 'the one that/who; what'
rikuʔahiʔ 'he was the one; that was the one' < riku- 'the one that' + ar- EV + ri- CONT.3.A + Ø 'be' + -Ø PERF

sections.heading: hii- 'other'
sections.content:
hii- 'other'
hiiraasa 'the other one (lying)' < hii- 'other' + ra- ABS + sa 'be lying' + -Ø SUB.4
tiibiiraaʔa 'this other one who came' < tii- 'this' + hii- 'other' + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV + a 'come' + -Ø SUB.4

sections.heading: Noun Incorporation

morphemes_mentioned.label: DU
morphemes_mentioned.notes: occurs at beginning of verb complex if noun referent is dual

morphemes_mentioned.label: PL
morphemes_mentioned.notes: immediately follows incorporated noun stem if noun referent is plural

sections.heading: Aspect and Subordination: Active and Passive Verbs
sections.content:
The aspect and subordination suffixes for active and passive verbs are given in their positions of occurrence in table 17.

Perfective aspect (PERF), non-subordinate. The perfective aspect, which generally designates a punctual or completed activity, has the form -his, except after a stem-final vowel plus k sequence (i.e., Vk), where it reduces to -s (see phonological rule 3R above). In non-subordinate perfective verb forms, where this aspect is in final position (i.e., where there is no following suffix), -his does not occur. If the stem ends in a consonant, there is no overt marking for this

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect, non-intentive, non-subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs in perfective, non-intentive, non-subordinate contexts.

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect, non-intentive, non-subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs when incorporated in perfective, non-intentive, non-subordinate contexts.

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect, non-intentive, subordinate, Class I
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs in perfective, non-intentive, subordinate contexts for Class I verbs.

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect, non-intentive, subordinate, Class II
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs in perfective, non-intentive, subordinate contexts for Class II verbs.

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect, non-intentive, subordinate, Class III
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs in perfective, non-intentive, subordinate contexts for Class III verbs.

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect, non-intentive, subordinate, Class IV
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs in perfective, non-intentive, subordinate contexts for Class IV verbs.

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect, intentive, non-subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Generally designates a punctual or completed activity. Reduces to -s after a stem-final Vk sequence. Does not occur in final position in non-subordinate perfective verb forms if no following suffix. Combines with -ta.

morphemes_mentioned.label: intentive
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -his for perfective, intentive, non-subordinate forms, and with -hus for imperfective, intentive, non-subordinate forms.

morphemes_mentioned.label: subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -his + -ta for perfective, intentive, subordinate forms, resulting in -bustarit. Combines with -hus + -ta for imperfective, intentive, subordinate forms, resulting in -bustarit.

morphemes_mentioned.label: imperfective aspect, non-intentive, non-subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs when incorporated in imperfective, non-intentive, non-subordinate contexts.

morphemes_mentioned.label: imperfective aspect, non-intentive, subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs in imperfective, non-intentive, subordinate contexts.

morphemes_mentioned.label: imperfective aspect, intentive/inchoative/habitual
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -ta, -itik, -uukuʔ, -uku for various imperfective categories.

morphemes_mentioned.label: inchoative
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -hus for imperfective, inchoative forms, resulting in -busitit.

morphemes_mentioned.label: habitual, non-subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -hus for imperfective, habitual, non-subordinate forms, resulting in -busuukuʔ.

morphemes_mentioned.label: habitual, subordinate
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -hus for imperfective, habitual, subordinate forms, resulting in -busuku.

morphemes_mentioned.label: perfective aspect
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Reduced form of -his after a stem-final vowel plus k (Vk) sequence.

tables.caption: Table 17. Suffixes for Aspect and Subordination

sections.content:
independent perfective form; but if it ends in a vowel, a glottal stop occurs after the vowel. If the perfective form of the verb has the intentive suffix (-ta INT) following it or if the perfective form of the verb is compounded with a following stem, -his does occur. Examples are:
tiburás ‘he found it’ < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + buras ‘find’ + Ø PERF; cp. tiburásista ‘he is going to find it’ < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + buras ‘find’ + -his PERF + -ta INT
tikíkat ‘he cried’ < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kikak ‘cry’ + Ø PERF; cp. tikikakísta ‘he is going to cry’ < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kikak ‘cry’ + -his PERF + -ta INT; takikaksaʔ ‘he came crying’ < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV.3.A + kikak ‘cry’ + -his PERF + a- ‘come’ + Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: PERF
morphemes_mentioned.notes: occurs when perfective form has intentive suffix or is compounded with a following stem

sections.heading: Perfective aspect, subordinate (SUB)
sections.content:
In active and passive verbs, however, subordinate perfective forms are marked in one of three ways: by one of two overt vowel suffixes, by a final-syllable change, or by no overt marking (i.e., Ø). Which subordinating suffix a verb takes is unpredictable, and so each active and passive verb stem belongs to one of four classes, each of which is defined by its subordinate perfective marker. These classes are given in table 14.
Class 1: raskuhisaaska ‘for you to call him’ < ra- INF.A + s- 2.A + ku- INF.B + bisaask ‘call’ + -a SUB.1
Class 2: ratkuburasi ‘for me to find him’ < ra- INF.A + t- 1.A + ku- INF.B + buras ‘find’ + -i SUB.2
A subclass of Class 2 verbs comprises the small set of verbs called i-stems, which have a stem-final vowel i that occurs in imperfective forms but not in perfective ones. In the base form of these stems that i is indicated by a stem-final -i, as in as-i (uur...) ‘to run’ and itk-i (ut...) ‘to dream about’. The principal parts of an i-stem are illustrated as follows:
uc-i ‘to be lying’: uc ‘be lying’ PERF; ucista ‘is going to lie’ PERF.INT; uci ‘be lying’ SUB.2; uciihuʔ ‘be lying’ IMPF; uciihusta ‘is going to be lying’ IMPF.INT; uciihu ‘be lying’ IMPF.SUB
Class 3: tiruʔ ‘he made it’ < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + raʔuk ‘make’ + Ø PERF; wiiraraʔu ‘when he made it’ < wii- ‘now’ + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + raʔuk ‘make’ + Ø SUB.3
Class 4: iriirakikat ‘where he cried’ < irii- ‘where’ + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + kikak ‘cry’ + Ø SUB.4

morphemes_mentioned.label: stem-final vowel
morphemes_mentioned.notes: occurs in imperfective forms of i-stems, not perfective

sections.heading: Imperfective aspect (IMPF), non-subordinate: -hus
sections.content: The imperfective aspect (IMPF) denotes an uncompleted activity or one in progress—i.e., one that expresses an action without denoting a beginning or end. In non-subordinate verbs the form...

sections.heading: GRAMMATICAL OVERVIEW (Imperfective aspect -:bus, continued)
sections.content:
of the imperfective is -:bus, where the colon preceding -:bus represents lengthening of the preceding, stem-final vowel. Here, too, the final s of -:bus is dropped when this suffix is the last element in a word, but is retained when -ta INT or any other morpheme immediately follows it. Examples:
tikacaakuʔ 'he is cutting it' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kacak 'cut, chop' + -:bus IMPF; cp. tikacat 'he cut it' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kacak 'cut' + Ø PERF
tikiikaahuʔ 'he is drinking it' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kiikaa 'drink' + -:bus IMPF; cp. tikiikaahusta 'he is going to be drinking it' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kiikaa 'drink' + -:bus IMPF + -ta INT; Tarahaʔ Takiihaahusaʔ 'Buffalo Comes to Drink' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV + kiikaa 'drink' + -:bus IMPF + a 'come' + Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: Imperfective aspect
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate. Colon indicates lengthening of preceding stem-final vowel. Final 's' dropped when word-final, retained when followed by -ta INT or other morphemes.

sections.heading: Imperfective aspect (IMPF), subordinate: -bus
sections.content:
The form of the imperfective in subordinate verb forms is -bus, the same as the non-subordinate form except that it does not lengthen the preceding stem-final vowel and, when the suffix is word-final, the final vowel (i.e., u) of -bus, which becomes -hu in its surface form, is not followed by a glottal stop, as it is in non-subordinate forms. Examples are:
raakuraarahuʔ 'for them to be coming' < ra- INF.A + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV + ku- INF.B + raar- 3.P.INDV.A + a 'come' + -:bus IMPF.SUB; cp. non-subordinate taraaraahuʔ 'they are coming'
iriirakiwakuʔ 'the one that barked' < irii- 'the one' + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + kiwak 'bark' + -:bus IMPF.SUB; cp. non-subordinate tikiwaakuʔ 'it's barking' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kiwak 'bark' + -:bus IMPF

morphemes_mentioned.label: Imperfective aspect
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Subordinate. Does not lengthen preceding stem-final vowel. Final 'u' becomes '-hu' when word-final and followed by a glottal stop, but the glottal stop is not present in subordinate forms.

sections.heading: Intentive aspect (INT), non-subordinate: -ta
sections.content:
The intentive aspect, represented by the form -ta, denotes an activity or state that is expected to occur and differs from a simple future tense in that there is no doubt or potentiality implied. It translates into English most commonly as '... is going to ...'. The suffix occurs in both perfective and imperfective forms, illustrated by the following:
taturaaʔiiwaatista 'I am going to tell a story' < ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + uur- PREV + raaʔiiwaat 'tell a story' + -his PERF + -ta INT
iriwiitataraahisaaskusta 'I am going to be calling you (by a certain kinship term)' < iri- 'that' + wii- 'now' + ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + a- 2.P + raahisaask 'call, address (by a kin term)' + -:bus IMPF + -ta INT; cp. iriwiitataraahisaaskuʔ 'I call you (by a certain kinship term)'

morphemes_mentioned.label: Intentive aspect
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Non-subordinate. Denotes expected activity/state, no doubt/potentiality. Translates as 'is going to'.

sections.heading: Intentive aspect (INT), subordinate: -rit
sections.content: In subordinate intentive forms, both perfective and imperfective, the intentive suffix -ta is always followed by -rit, which marks the verb as a subordinate form. Its occurrence is illustrated by the following pair of words: [No examples provided on this page]

morphemes_mentioned.label: Intentive aspect
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Subordinate. Always follows -ta in subordinate intentive forms.

sections.heading: Subordinate Clauses (continued)
sections.content:
wiiruuhaasitiiʔ 'when it is going to occur' < wii- 'now' + ri- CONT.3.A + ut- PREV + raasiktik 'happen, occur' + -his PERF + -ta INT + -rit INT.SUB;
wiiruuhaasitiiʔkustarit 'when it is going to be happening' < wii- 'now' + ri- CONT.3.A + ut- PREV + raasiktik 'happen, occur' + -hus IMPF + -ta INT + -rit INT.SUB

examples.translation: when it is going to occur
when it is going to be happening

sections.heading: Inchoative aspect (INCH)
sections.content: The inchoative aspect denotes an activity that is beginning to occur or is in an inceptive state. It is marked by the suffix -itik, which is always preceded by the imperfective suffix, and is translated into English most commonly as either ‘beginning’ or ‘starting’ an activity. Examples are:

morphemes_mentioned.label: INCH
morphemes_mentioned.notes: suffix, always preceded by the imperfective suffix

sections.heading: Habitual aspect (HAB)
sections.content: The habitual aspect is represented by -uukuʔ/-uku, which always comes immediately after the imperfective aspectual marker. In non-subordinate verbs the habitual has a long vowel uu and there is a glottal stop following the final u, while in subordinate forms there is no final glottal stop and the initial vowel of the morpheme is usually short. The habitual aspect denotes activities that occur customarily or out of habit and are translated into English as ‘used to do’ or ‘usually do’. Examples are:

morphemes_mentioned.label: HAB
morphemes_mentioned.notes: suffix, non-subordinate forms, long vowel uu, glottal stop, immediately after imperfective marker

morphemes_mentioned.label: HAB
morphemes_mentioned.notes: suffix, subordinate forms, short vowel, no glottal stop, immediately after imperfective marker

examples.translation: when they went to gather them
the one who used to announce (i.e., camp crier)

sections.heading: Aspect and Subordination: Descriptive and Locative Verbs
sections.content: The forms for existential and inchoative forms of descriptive and locative verbs differ from those of active and passive verbs, although there is certainly overlap between categories and morphemes. Both classes of verbs make a basic aspectual distinction between existential and inchoative forms, and each of these in turn distinguishes between simple and intentive forms. Inchoative forms of both classes also distinguish between perfective and imperfective forms. Both classes also have separate non-subordinate and subordinate forms.

sections.heading: Existential Forms: Descriptive Verbs
sections.content:
Simple existential descriptive forms. The simple existential forms of descriptive verbs comprise a descriptive stem followed by the verb uk 'to exist, be', which here functions as an inflectional suffix. In non-subordinate forms uk is a null form when it is word final. There are two classes of descriptive verbs based on their surface forms:

Class I descriptive verbs, which are the most numerous, are characterized by a reduplication of the vowel in their final syllable when the forms are non-subordinate. The rule for this reduplication is:

Vᵢ(Vᵢ) → Vᵢ(Vᵢ)²Vᵢ / C _ C + uk (+ NON-SUB)

tipahaaʔat 'it is red' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + pahaat 'be red' + -uk 'be'; cp. iriirapahaattu 'the one that is red'

tihuraabtarariʔit 'the ground is burned off' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + huraar- 'ground' + tararit 'be burned, scorched' + -uk 'be'; cp. iriiratarariitu 'where it is scorched'

Class II descriptive verbs comprise stems that do not undergo the final-syllable vocalic reduplication that Class I verbs do, illustrated by:

tiwaarukstiiʔ 'he is holy' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + waarukstii 'be holy' + -uk 'be'; cp. iriwaaarukstiiʔu 'for one to be holy'

takuctaris 'the bucket is heavy' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + a- PREV + kuc- 'bucket' + taris 'be heavy' + -uk 'be'

Intentive existential descriptive verbs. These intentives are formed by suffixing -ta INT to the perfective form of descriptive stems (i.e., STEM + -uk 'be' + -his PERF + -ta, which results in STEM + -uksta). The following are examples:

tiwakarahiisuksta 'he is going to have a raspy voice' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + wakarahiis 'be raspy voiced' + -uk 'be' + -his PERF + -ta INT

tipahaatuksta 'it is going to be red' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + pahaat 'be red' + uk 'be' + -his PERF + -ta INT

Subordinate existential descriptive verbs. The subordinate form of existential descriptive verbs is represented by the subordinate form of uk 'to be', which is -u in word-final position. Examples:

iriirapahaattu 'the one that is red' < irii- 'the one' + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + pahaat 'red' + -uk SUB.D

morphemes_mentioned.label: existential verb
morphemes_mentioned.notes: functions as an inflectional suffix, 'to exist, be'; null in non-subordinate word-final forms

morphemes_mentioned.label: reduplication rule
morphemes_mentioned.notes: for Class I descriptive verbs, final syllable vowel reduplication

sections.content: iriirawakarahiisu 'the one who has a raspy voice' < irii- 'the one' + ra- ABS + Ø- 3.A + wakarahiis 'be raspy voiced' + -uk SUB.D

sections.heading: Inchoative Forms: Descriptive Verbs
sections.content: Descriptive verbs form the inchoative with the suffix -aar 'become, get' (which is also an independent verb) and by changing their inflectional class from active to passive. The principal forms of -aar as a suffix for descriptive verbs are:

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: 'become, get', forms inchoative from descriptive verbs, changes inflectional class from active to passive

tables.caption: Principal forms of -aar as a suffix for descriptive verbs

sections.heading: Existential Forms: Locative Verbs
sections.content: Locative verbs make the same aspectual distinctions as descriptive verbs. They are distinguished in part by the suffix -wi that occurs on all inflected forms except for the simple non-subordinate. The distinctions and forms expressing them are presented below.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Distinguishes locative verbs, occurs on all inflected forms except simple non-subordinate

sections.content:
• Simple existential locative forms. Existential locative verbs in non-subordinate forms have no overt marking on the verb. Examples are:
tiikita aruusaʔ 'he is riding the horse' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + ikita 'be astride' + Ø PERF
tikawihaahka 'the projectile is on (i.e., attached to) the end of the shaft' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + kawihaar- 'projectile' + ka 'be on' + Ø PERF

• Intentive existential locative verbs. The intentive existential form has the sequence -wi + -his PERF + -ta INT (that becomes -wista) suffixed to the locative stem. Example:
tuciraahawista 'it is going to be tilted' < ti- IND + Ø- 3.A + ut- PREV + tiraaha 'be tilted' + -wi SUB.L + -his PERF + -ta INT

• Subordinate existential locative verbs. Subordinate locative verbs take the suffix -wi, as in the following example:
rakukatawi 'to be against' < ra- INF.A + Ø- 3.A + ku- INF.A + kata 'be against (a vertical surface)' + -wi SUB.L

sections.heading: Inchoative Forms: Locative Verbs
sections.content: Locative verbs form their five inchoative aspects with the suffixes -wi followed by -itik INCH. This aspect describes an agent that is assuming or changing into the locative state that the stem denotes.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Suffix used in forming inchoative aspects, preceding -itik.

morphemes_mentioned.label: INCH (Inchoative) suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Inchoative (INCH) suffix, follows -wi in forming inchoative aspects.

morphemes_mentioned.label: zero morpheme
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -itik for Perfective (PERF) and Perfective Subordinate (PERF.SUB) aspects.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -itik and -ta for Perfective Interrogative (PERF.INT) aspect.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -itik and -his for Perfective Interrogative (PERF.INT) aspect.

morphemes_mentioned.label: suffix
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Combines with -itik for Imperfective (IMPF) and Imperfective Subordinate (IMPF.SUB) aspects.

tables.caption: Inchoative Aspect Forms of Locative Verbs

sections.heading: Grammatical Overview
sections.content:
Skiri is polysynthetic in its structure in that words -- primarily verbs -- are composed of an unusually large number of meaningful elements (morphemes) that generally in other, non-polysynthetic languages are separate, or independent, words. Most of the categories marked on the Skiri verb are grammatical in nature and include mode, aspect, person, tense, possession, benefaction, and other such categories. Also generally incorporated into the verb is the noun patient of a transitive verb or the noun agent of an intransitive one. Thus in Skiri the verb is the most elaborate, as well as prevalent, word class, and verbs translate into English as either a clause or a sentence. Nouns constitute the only other word class that allows inflection, but in a very limited manner.

Generally a freely occurring verb has at least five morphemes comprising it, but often as many as ten or more morphemes. Those morphemes are tightly bound together by a complex set of phonological rules (presented in chapter 3) that change their forms and thereby 'disguise' their underlying forms or identities. Languages of this type, in which morphemes fuse or change their forms to one extent or another when coming together in word building, are often designated as fusional.

sections.heading: Nouns
sections.content:
In Skiri there are two types of noun stems, independent and dependent. The independent stem is a noun that stands freely, with or without the nominal absolutive suffix -uʔ (see below). A dependent stem, in contrast, is one that cannot by itself stand independently as a word. To become a free form it must have a suffix, although generally it occurs only in compounds.

Examples of dependent nouns (cited as N-DEP herein) are:
siis- 'sharp, pointed object': siiski 'awl' (< siis- + -kis DIM); siistacaraʔ 'fork' (< siis- + -taar PL + taraa 'be side by side')
asaa- 'horse; dog': asaaki 'dog' (< asaa- + -kis DIM); asiicapaat 'mare' (< asaa- + icapaak 'woman; female'). The independent form of 'horse' is aruusaʔ.

sections.heading: Noun Derivation
sections.content: There are five derivational affixes in Skiri, two of which do not add new semantic content to noun stems, but serve only to form independent nouns. The other three, one a suffix and two prefixes, do add content. These affixes are given in table 5.

morphemes_mentioned.label: Diminutive (DIM)
morphemes_mentioned.notes: asaakiʔ 'dog' < asaa- 'dog; horse' + -kis DIM; riicikiʔ 'knife' < riici- 'knife' + kis DIM

sections.heading: Verbs
sections.content:
The basic component of active verb stems is the root, usually a monosyllabic but sometimes a di- or tri-syllabic morpheme. In Skiri there are approximately one hundred such roots, and consequently the vast majority of active verb stems are derived forms. Stems are formed by the addition of verbal prefixes; by compounding locative, descriptive, or noun stems, and combinations of those elements to verb roots; and by combining preverbs with roots and stems. The root hak 'to pass, go by', for example, has hundreds of different stems based on it, and the root at 'to go' has even more.

Many active and passive verb stems in Skiri are discontinuous stems, in which the stem base is preceded by a preverb, a monosyllabic morpheme that occurs among the inner prefixes preceding the stem base but is usually not contiguous with it. There are three preverbs in Skiri: ir-/a-, uur- and ut-. Each of them is identical in form to grammatical prefixes that have other functions (see below), but when they occur as parts of stems they do not have any tangible meaning.

Example: wiitatiiksaʔ 'I have come' < wii- 'now' + ta- IND.1/2.A + t- 1.A + ir- PREV + uks- AOR + a 'come' + -Ø PERF

morphemes_mentioned.label: preverb
morphemes_mentioned.notes: ir- alternates with a- for 3rd person agent

sections.heading: Person and number.
sections.content: Pronominal agent and patient prefixes occur immediately following the modal prefixes [table 7:11, 12, 15]. Skiri distinguishes two persons—first and second—as well as three numbers—singular, dual, and plural. In both the dual and plural numbers there is a further distinction between inclusive (including the person spoken to) and exclusive (excluding the person spoken to) [table 7:11-12, 15]. There is, in addition, an obviative (OBV) prefix ir- that designates an indefinite third-person agent or a reversal in third-person agent and patient [table 7:13].

morphemes_mentioned.label: obviative (OBV) prefix
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: designates an indefinite third-person agent or a reversal in third-person agent and patient
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Occurs as an agent prefix for obviative forms, and as a patient prefix for obviative forms (in combination with a-)

morphemes_mentioned.label: DU (dual number)
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: dual number for both agent and patient
morphemes_mentioned.notes: precedes the modal prefix; also part of the combination si- DU and ir- COLL.PL

morphemes_mentioned.label: plural agent
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: first and second person agents in active, descriptive, and locative verbs

morphemes_mentioned.label: collective plural prefix
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: third person plural agents that are a group or single entity in active intransitive, descriptive, and locative verbs
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Distinct from the obviative ir-; also part of the combination si- DU and ir- COLL.PL

morphemes_mentioned.label: individuative plural prefix
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: third person plural agents that are individuals acting or existing separately in active intransitive, descriptive, and locative verbs
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Also used for 3.INAN.P (third person inanimate plural patient)

morphemes_mentioned.label: 3.P.COLL.A (third person collective plural agent)
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Used in active intransitive, descriptive, and locative verbs

morphemes_mentioned.label: 3.P.INDV.A (third person individuative plural agent)
morphemes_mentioned.notes: Used in active intransitive, descriptive, and locative verbs

sections.content: iriiruurakuuraaru 'whatever the kind of animal was,' that is, 'all kinds of animals' < irii- 'the one' + ruu- 'there' + ra- INF.A + Ø- 3.A + ku- INF.B + i- SEQ + raburaar- 'animal' + -u SUB.D

tables.caption: Table 15. The Verb Stem Template
rows: ri-, ra-, ruu- PORT; cir-, ciras- COM

sections.heading: Suffixes
sections.content:
Only two categories are marked by suffixes: aspect and subordination. Active and stative verbs occur in one of two basic aspects, perfective and imperfective. There are five other aspects that are built on one or both of these basic aspects: an intentive, which indicates that something is going to happen; a habitual, which indicates that something regularly happens; and an inchoative, which indicates that something is beginning to happen. All verbs occur in both independent and subordinate forms. Independent forms do not take a suffix, whereas subordinate forms do take one.

Each of the major classes of verbs is, in part, defined by its distinctive subordinate suffix, which defines its class. Descriptive stems, for example, take the subordinate suffix -u, while locative verbs take the suffix -wi. Active and stative verbs, in contrast, belong to one of four subclasses, of which two are marked by different vowel suffixes, one by a final-syllable stress change, and one by the lack of a suffix.

morphemes_mentioned.label: subordinate suffix (descriptive)
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: marks subordinate form of descriptive verbs

morphemes_mentioned.label: subordinate suffix (locative)
morphemes_mentioned.meaning: marks subordinate form of locative verbs

tables.caption: Table 16. Verb Suffix Template
rows: PERFECTIVE / PERFECTIVE SUBORDINATE / IMPERFECTIVE / IMPERFECTIVE SUBORDINATE
